steps.title: Identify the missing measure
steps.content: Harper et al.'s research question is whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions. The team already calculated, for each extended-brand pair, how purchase probability changed when the same brand was purchased in the other category. The missing measure is how similar consumers perceive the paired product categories to be.

steps.title: Connect the right variables
steps.content: The useful design must connect the two variables named in the research question: perceived similarity between the product categories and the already calculated change in purchase probability for each extended-brand pair. Studies focused on other variables would not show whether category similarity predicts purchasing behavior.

steps.title: Rule out mismatched variables
steps.content:
• Choice B - Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition of each brand in the extended-brand pairs: Brand recognition is not perceived category similarity, and average cost is not purchase probability.
  • Choice C - Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of one product in each extended-brand pair to other products in the same category: This measures similarity within one category, not similarity between the two categories in an extended-brand pair.

steps.title: Reject the remaining mismatch
steps.content: Choice D - Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition for each brand in the extended-brand pairs. This still measures brand recognition rather than category similarity, and it compares that recognition with purchase frequency from a different group of households. Choice A - Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair - is the design that tests the stated research question. The answer is A.
